data.title: Is It Worth Considering Nedap N.V. (AMS:NEDAP) For Its Upcoming Dividend?
data.description:
Some investors rely on dividends for growing their wealth, and if you're one of those dividend sleuths, you might be intrigued to know that Nedap N.V. (AMS:NEDAP) is about to go ex-dividend in just two days. The ex-dividend date is commonly two business days before the record date, which is the cut-off date for shareholders to be present on the company's books to be eligible for a dividend payment. The ex-dividend date is important because any transaction on a stock needs to have been settled before the record date in order to be eligible for a dividend. Accordingly, Nedap investors that purchase the stock on or after the 10th of April will not receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 20th of April.

The company's next dividend payment will be €3.70 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of €3.70 to shareholders. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that Nedap has a trailing yield of 4.6% on the current share price of €81.20. If you buy this business for its dividend, you should have an idea of whether Nedap's dividend is reliable and sustainable. That's why we should always check whether the dividend payments appear sustainable, and if the company is growing.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. Nedap paid out 99% of its earnings, which is more than we're comfortable with, unless there are mitigating circumstances. That said, even highly profitable companies sometimes might not generate enough cash to pay the dividend, which is why we should always check if the dividend is covered by cash flow. Dividends consumed 61% of the company's free cash flow last year, which is within a normal range for most dividend-paying organisations.

It's good to see that while Nedap's dividends were not well covered by profits, at least they are affordable from a cash perspective. Still, if the company continues paying out such a high percentage of its profits, the dividend could be at risk if business turns sour.

View our latest analysis for Nedap

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. Fortunately for readers, Nedap's earnings per share have been growing at 12% a year for the past five years.

Many investors will assess a company's dividend performance by evaluating how much the dividend payments have changed over time. Nedap has delivered 11% dividend growth per year on average over the past 10 years. Both per-share earnings and dividends have both been growing rapidly in recent times, which is great to see.

Final Takeaway

Is Nedap worth buying for its dividend? Nedap has been growing its earnings per share nicely, although judging by the difference between its profit and cashflow payout ratios, the company might have reported some write-offs over the last year. While it does have some good things going for it, we're a bit ambivalent and it would take more to convince us of Nedap's dividend merits.

However if you're still interested in Nedap as a potential investment, you should definitely consider some of the risks involved with Nedap. In terms of investment risks, we've identified 1 warning sign with Nedap and understanding them should be part of your investment process.

data.title: Assessing Collective Mining (TSX:CNL) Valuation After High Grade Ramp Zone Drill Results
data.description:
Collective Mining (TSX:CNL) is back in focus after fresh Ramp Zone drill results at the Apollo system highlighted several high grade gold and silver intercepts and expanded the known mineralized envelope for investors to assess.

See our latest analysis for Collective Mining.

The fresh Ramp Zone drill results come on the heels of a wider re rating, with Collective Mining’s share price climbing to CA$25.81 and recording a 30.09% 90 day share price return alongside a very large 3 year total shareholder return. Price to Book of 11.8x: Is it justified?

Collective Mining trades on a P/B of 11.8x, which looks cheap against its close peer group but expensive compared to the wider Canadian metals and mining space.

The P/B ratio compares the CA$2.34b market value to the company’s book value of equity and is often used for pre revenue or loss making resource names where earnings based multiples are not very helpful. For Collective Mining, this means investors are currently paying 11.8 times the accounting value of its net assets.

Against its direct peers, that 11.8x P/B is described as good value, with the peer average sitting far higher at 134.8x. That suggests the market is attaching a lower premium to Collective Mining’s exploration portfolio than to some comparable names, even after a share price that is up 30.09% over 90 days and a very large 3 year total shareholder return.

Step back to the broader Canadian metals and mining industry, though, and the picture flips, with the sector average P/B at 3.2x. On this wider view, Collective Mining screens as expensive, trading at more than 3 times the industry average multiple. This implies the market is assigning a much richer valuation than it typically does for the sector. See what the numbers say about this price — find out in our valuation breakdown.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Buying Five Below (FIVE) After Its 1 Year 284% Surge?
data.description:
• Some investors may be wondering whether Five Below's share price still offers value after a strong run, or if most of the potential upside is already reflected in the current price.
  • The stock last closed at US$233.72, with returns of 6.3% over 7 days, 8.6% over 30 days, 20.8% year to date and 283.9% over 1 year. This hints that the market has been reassessing the business.
  • Recent coverage has focused on the stock's sharp 1 year gain and what it might indicate about investor expectations for the retailer, including whether enthusiasm is starting to run ahead of fundamentals. At the same time, some commentary has questioned whether shoppers will keep supporting the current store format at the pace implied by the recent share price.
  • Despite this track record, Five Below currently has a valuation score of 0 out of 6. The next step is to compare what different valuation methods suggest and then look at a more complete way to think about value that will be covered at the end of this article.

Five Below scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Five Below Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value. For Five Below, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach that starts from the latest reported Free Cash Flow of about $260.7m.

Analysts and extrapolations feed into a path where projected Free Cash Flow reaches around $530.5m in 2035, according to Simply Wall St’s ten year projections. These figures, all in US$, are then discounted back to today using the model’s required rate of return assumptions.

On this basis, the estimated intrinsic value from the DCF comes out at about $137.72 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $233.72, the model implies the stock is around 69.7% overvalued based on these cash flow assumptions.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Five Below may be overvalued by 69.7%. Discover 62 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Five Below.

Approach 2: Five Below Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a common way to think about value because it links what you pay for the stock to the earnings the business is generating today. A “normal” or “fair” P/E will usually be higher for companies where investors expect stronger earnings growth and are comfortable with the risks, and lower when growth expectations are more modest or risks are higher.

Five Below currently trades on a P/E of 36.0x. That sits above both the Specialty Retail industry average of about 20.0x and a peer group average of 28.2x, suggesting investors are currently willing to pay a higher price for each dollar of earnings than these benchmarks.

Simply Wall St also provides a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 19.9x for Five Below. This goes further than a simple peer or industry comparison because it factors in the company’s earnings growth profile, its industry, profit margins, market cap and identified risks. On this yardstick, Five Below’s current P/E of 36.0x is well above the Fair Ratio, which points to the shares screening as expensive on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 20 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Five Below Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. On Simply Wall St's Community page you can use Narratives, which let you set out a clear story for Five Below, link that story to specific revenue, earnings and margin forecasts, turn those forecasts into a Fair Value, and compare that Fair Value to the current share price to help you think about when to buy or sell. You can then see that view update automatically when new news or earnings arrive. For example, one Narrative might lean toward the higher fair value of about US$305 with stronger growth and margins, while another might sit closer to the lower end near US$109 with more cautious assumptions about store expansion, digital progress and competition.

For Five Below however we'll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Five Below Narratives:

🐂 Five Below Bull Case

Fair value: US$305.00

Implied discount to this fair value: about 23.3% compared to the last close.

Revenue growth assumption: 12.3% a year.

  • Assumes Five Below can use store expansion toward a potential 3,500 locations, higher price points through Five Beyond, and supply chain efficiencies to support higher margins over time.
  • Builds in stronger revenue and earnings forecasts than consensus, with higher profit margins and a future P/E of around 36.0x to justify the US$305.00 fair value.
  • Flags meaningful risks around reliance on physical stores, tariff exposure on imported goods, competition, and an underdeveloped digital presence that could challenge this upbeat view.

🐻 Five Below Bear Case

Fair value: US$229.32

Implied premium to this fair value: about 1.9% compared to the last close.

Revenue growth assumption: 11.8% a year.

  • Builds a more balanced outlook that still assumes solid revenue growth, but with tighter margins and a lower earnings profile than the bullish case.
  • Uses a consensus style fair value of about US$229.32 based on earnings growing and the company trading on a future P/E near 35.0x.
